coredata.dc:title: Polystyrene foam-based pipette-tip Micro-solid phase extraction (PFPT-μSPE) using Ni-MOF@S-CQDs nanocomposite: A novel method for efficient extraction of Lead from water and food samples

coredata.dc:description: A novel Ni-MOF@S-CQDs nanocomposite was synthesized directly into the pores of a polystyrene foam. It was characterized using FTIR, XRD, SEM, and SEM-EDX techniques. The adsorbent was effectively applied for the extraction of Pb (II) from water, juice, and iced tea samples, followed by analysis using UV-VIS spectrometry. Various analytical factors, including pH, adsorbent amount, adsorption and desorption times, and sample volume, were studied. Under the optimum conditions, the analytical features were obtained; the LDR of 50–5000 μg L−1, the LOD of 15 μg L−1, and the RSDs% of 2.0 and 4.0 for intra-day and inter-day, respectively. The preconcentration (PF) and the enrichment (EF) factors of 30, the factor of 28.5, and the extraction efficiency (EE) of 95 % were obtained. Certified reference materials (CRMs) were used to validate the novel Polystyrene Foam-Pipette Tip Micro-Solid Phase Extraction (PFPT-μSPE) technique. This study is eco-friendly, accurate, and economical for the extraction of Pb (II) from water and beverage samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel Ni-MOF@S-CQDs nanocomposite was synthesized directly into the pores of a polystyrene foam. It was characterized using FTIR, XRD, SEM, and SEM-EDX techniques. The adsorbent was effectively applied for the extraction of Pb (II) from water, juice, and iced tea samples, followed by analysis using UV-VIS spectrometry. Various analytical factors, including pH, adsorbent amount, adsorption and desorption times, and sample volume, were studied. Under the optimum conditions, the analytical features were obtained; the LDR of 50–5000 μg L−1, the LOD of 15 μg L−1, and the RSDs% of 2.0 and 4.0 for intra-day and inter-day, respectively. The preconcentration (PF) and the enrichment (EF) factors of 30, the factor of 28.5, and the extraction efficiency (EE) of 95 % were obtained. Certified reference materials (CRMs) were used to validate the novel Polystyrene Foam-Pipette Tip Micro-Solid Phase Extraction (PFPT-μSPE) technique. This study is eco-friendly, accurate, and economical for the extraction of Pb (II) from water and beverage samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lead (Pb), a prevalent and highly poisonous heavy metal, presents significant health and environmental hazards even in minimal amounts (Habila, Alothman, & Soylak, 2015; Mdluli, Nomngongo, & Mketo, 2025; Raj & Das, 2023). Pb (II), a non-biodegradable element, accumulates in biological systems and is linked to neurological problems, renal dysfunction, and developmental abnormalities, especially in children (Mrozik, Rajaeifar, Heidrich, & Christensen, 2021; Soylak & Cay, 2007). The World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have set the maximum allowable quantity of Pb (II) in drinking water at 10 μg L−1 (Bat & Öztekin, 2025; Ebrahimi et al., 2020; Soylak, Jagirani, & Uzcan, 2022). The increasing concentrations of Pb (II) in natural water bodies and food products, attributable to industrial effluents, agricultural runoff, and aging plumbing systems, necessitate the development of rapid, reliable, and cost-effective detection technologies (Demirel, Tuzen, Saracoglu, & Soylak, 2008; Salman, Znad, Hasan, & Hasan, 2021; Soylak & Karaca, 2016; Soylak, Yilmaz, Ghaedi, Montazerozohori, & Sheibani, 2012).

Conventional analytical techniques, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Vicentino, Cassella, Leite, & Resano, 2020), graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (GFAAS) (de Oliveira, Augusto Peres, Lurdes Felsner, & Cristiane Justi, 2017), and flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) (Salamat, Elci, & Elci, 2024), are widely utilized for the detection of Pb (II). Although these techniques exhibit high sensitivity and accuracy, they are constrained by considerable limitations, such as high operational costs, complex sample preparation, dependence on expensive instruments, and unsuitability for on-site or routine screening applications. The recognized constraints have prompted the exploration of compact, cost-effective, and environmentally sustainable solutions.

Recently, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have garnered interest as novel materials for sample preparation and adsorption, owing to their remarkably high surface area, tunable porosity, and diverse surface functions (Elzain Hassan Ahmed & Soylak, 2024). Ni-based MOFs are especially appealing due to their structural stability, accessible metal sites, and surface chemistry conducive to chelation (Furukawa, Cordova, O'Keeffe, & Yaghi, 2013; Salamat & Soylak, 2024b). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon-rich nanomaterials known for their tunable fluorescence, high surface functionality, and environmental safety (Sharma & Chowdhury, 2025). Although their primary particle size is often below 10 nm, they may appear as larger agglomerates, particularly in dried or surface-deposited forms such as those observed in scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Unlike conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), CQDs are generally metal-free and exhibit low toxicity, making them suitable for green analytical applications. The hybridization of CQDs, particularly sulfur-doped CQDs (S-CQDs), results in a composite material that exhibits improved electron transport, heightened hydrophilicity, and supplementary π-π and electrostatic interaction sites (Rani, Ng, Ng, & Mahmoudi, 2020). S-CQDs introduce sulfur-containing functional groups (e.g., –SO3H, –SH), enhancing their affinity for heavy metals such as Pb (II).

Solid-Phase Extraction (SPE) is a widely used sample preparation technique that relies on the selective retention of analytes on a solid sorbent, offering improved cleanup and preconcentration over traditional liquid–liquid extraction methods (Yilmaz & Soylak, 2014). Micro-Solid Phase Extraction (μ-SPE) is a miniaturized adaptation of SPE that utilizes smaller sorbent amounts and solvent volumes while maintaining high selectivity and enrichment efficiency (Soylak, Çoban, & Ahmed, 2024; Soylak, Salamat, & Uzcan, 2025). The μSPE technique's benefits include ease of use, speed, better sample cleanup, precise analysis, and minimal or no consumption of organic solvents (Jagirani & Soylak, 2024). Pipette-Tip Micro-Solid Phase Extraction (PT-μSPE) has garnered considerable interest for the efficient application of these materials in a practical extraction format. PT-μSPE is a compact sample preparation method that provides minimal solvent usage, brief extraction durations, and the possibility for automation (Turoňová, Kujovská Krčmová, & Švec, 2021). A variant of this methodology, termed Polystyrene Foam-Pipette Tip Micro-Solid Phase Extraction (PFPT-μSPE), enhances dispersion and interaction between the sorbent and analyte, rendering it appropriate for trace-level analyses in intricate matrices.

In this work, we present a novel composite adsorbent based on Ni-MOF and S-CQDs immobilized on a polystyrene foam substrate for PFPT-μSPE of Pb (II). This material integrates the extensive surface area and coordination sites of Ni-MOF with the functional versatility of S-CQDs, resulting in a robust affinity and selectivity for Pb (II) ions. PADAP (2-(5-Bromo-2-pyridylazo)-5-(diethylamino)phenol) was chosen as a complexing agent due to its robust and specific affinity for Pb (II) at neutral pH, resulting in a stable colored complex that can be quantified by UV-VIS spectrometry.

The rationale for employing UV-VIS detection is its accessibility, affordability, and alignment with the concepts of green analytical chemistry (Aritonang, Kojong, Koleangan, & Wuntu, 2021). When combined with PFPT-μSPE and PADAP, UV–Vis facilitates the accurate quantification of Pb (II) without requiring complex instrumentation. The devised approach seeks to establish an environmentally sustainable, effective, and reproducible framework for the detection of trace Pb (II) in water, juice, and tea samples. This study aims to synthesize, characterize, and utilize a Ni-MOF@S-CQDs polystyrene foam composite for the extraction and quantification of Pb (II) by PFPT-μSPE and UV-VIS spectrometry. The method's analytical performance, selectivity, repeatability, and application to real samples are systematically assessed and analyzed.

The investigation used only analytically graded substances. Sigma-Aldrich provided nitric acid (HNO3), terephthalic acid (TPA), and 5-Amino-1,3,4-thiadiazole-2-thiol (5-ATDAT). Aluminum foil used for covering the tips was purchased from Sera Aluminum Foil (Kayseri, Turkey). Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), glucose, and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were all obtained from MERCK (Darmstadt, Germany). Micropipettes were procured from ISO lab Laborgerate GmbH, while the weighing balance was provided by BAYKON company with model numbers AS220 and S/N 462201. 2-(5-Bromo-2-pyridylazo)-5-(diethylamino)phenol (PADAP) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. HANNA edge pH meter was used for observation and adjustment of pH. Analyte absorbance was measured using the Hitachi UH 5300 Double Beam Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, USA) equipped with a quartz cuvette system. Filling the sample and reference cells with methanol and then operating the instrument should adjust the baseline for the wavelength range before measuring the samples. Centrifuge provided by Hettich ROTOFIX 32 having 208-240 V, 50–60 HZ using 160 W were used for real sample preparation. A PerkinElmer 00 FT-IR spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA) covering the wavenumber range of 4000 to 500 cm−1 was used for Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), and an instrument with step size 0.262606 from XRD-BRUKER AXS D8 ADVANCE, Türkiye, was used for X-ray diffraction (XRD).

Initially, 2.32 g of Ni (NO3)2·6H2O and 0.66 g of TPA were dissolved in 50 mL of DMF solution. The mixture was then subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min to get a uniformly dispersed suspension. The suspension was subsequently placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a 70 mL capacity. Upon sealing the autoclave, it was exposed to a temperature of 180 °C for 24 h and allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature. After cooling, the resulting green product underwent three aqueous washes and three ethanol washes. The product was then dried in an oven for 24 h at 90 °C (Gao et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Sulfur-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots (S-CQDs) Nanosphere
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of S-CQDs was performed utilizing a modified hydrothermal method according to the outlined protocol (Liu, Sun, Ke Zhen, Jing Liang, & XiaoYu, 2024). Initially, 0.50 g of 5-ATDAT and 0.45 g of glucose were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. The resulting solution was subsequently subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min. The solution was agitated for approximately thirty minutes. The mixture was subsequently transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to heating for 12 h at 120 °C. Following the cooling process, the synthesized S-CQDs were collected, centrifuged, and washed with water and ethanol to eliminate contaminants. The synthesized substance was dried for 24 h after the process.

For the synthesis of the nanocomposite, firstly, 2.32 g of Ni (NO3)2.6H2O and 0.66 g of TPA were separately dissolved in 30 mL of DMF solution, referred to as Solutions A and B, respectively. After that, Solution B was poured slowly into Solution A. Finally, the resulting mixture was stirred until it was homogenous, along with the dropwise addition the 0.5 mL of the concentrated HCl solution. Next, 1.0 g of synthesized S-CQD was added to the solution, followed by stirring for 10 min. After that, 0.5 g of polystyrene foam and the prepared mixture were placed into a Teflon container, followed by heating at 180 °C for 24 h. Afterward, the synthesized nanocomposite was cooled to room temperature and then washed sequentially with water and ethanol before being dried in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h (Fig. 1 A).

Battery wastewater from two industries in Kayseri, Turkey, and two samples of tap water from two different locations of Erciyes University in Kayseri, Turkey, were included in the sample utilized to study the target analytes. Additionally, samples of mixed fruit juice, apricot juice, and iced tea in watermelon and peach flavors were purchased from the Kayseri local market. After centrifugation and filtration steps to prepare them, the samples' pH was adjusted to 7.0 using a 5.0 M NaOH solution. After that, all these actual samples underwent an optimized extraction process. The Pb content was determined using UV-VIS spectrometry.

The PFPT-μSPE approach was implemented by placing 20 mg of adsorbent within a pipette tip, which was covered with aluminum foil to block the light interaction. The tip was then thoroughly washed using a buffer solution of pH = 7.0 to trigger the adsorbent activity. Afterward, 10 μL of PADAP (ligand) was injected within the tip and allowed to interact with the adsorbent for 5–10 min to ease ligand attachment. Then, a mixture of the analyte and buffer solution was passed into the pipette tip and incubated for up to four cycles to enhance analyte adsorption on the surface of the adsorbent. Then, by using 1.0 mL of 3.0 M HNO3 solution, the tips were splashed, and this step was repeated up to 10 times, so the analyte was properly desorbed from the surface of the adsorbent. The eluent was then analyzed via UV-VIS spectrometry at 560 nm wavelength (Fig. 1B). This technique, authorized for efficient Pb extraction, offers dependability, sensitivity, and prospective applications in industrial, environmental, and biological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enrichment factor, Preconcentration factor, and extraction recovery

Using a range of distinct concentrations of 0.5–5.0 mg L−1, two calibration curves were plotted as post-extraction and pre-extraction, respectively. Then the EF was calculated. The preconcentration factor of the tested components was calculated using eq. (2).

(Salamat & Soylak, 2024a). The studies were conducted on aqueous sample solutions and replicated three times. The extraction of Pb metal ions involves using a sample solution volume of 10 mL and a final extraction phase volume of 1.0 mL. These parameters were consistent across all experimental methods.

The identification of functional groups within a composite was analyzed by using FTIR (Fig. 2A) in the spectrum of Ni-MOF, a sharp peak from the organic linker and metal-ligand coordination is exhibited. The peak in the range of 3262 cm−1 indicates the OH or NH groups, while a peak in the range of 1557–1699 cm−1 demonstrates CO stretching vibrations, Carboxylate or amide groups. The peaks below 1000 cm−1 like 811 and 579, are specific for metal-ligand vibrations, notably NiN or NiO bonds (Li, Tao, Luo, Xie, & Tu, 2023; Venkatesan, Shanmugam, & Arumugam, 2024). The peak around 1195 cm−1 in the S-CQD spectrum illustrates sulfur functionality, so this peak confirms the presence of sulfur functionality. The peak in the region of 3000–3500 cm−1 is an indication of the presence of hydroxyl or carboxyl groups. Moreover, CC or CO peaks lie in the range of 1557–1699 cm−1, typical for aromatic or conjugated systems observed in carbon quantum dots (Mir, Malik, Shukla, Singh, & Kumar, 2024; Zhou et al., 2017). The composite spectrum of Ni-MOF@S-CQDs retains the characteristics of both Ni-MOF and S-CQDs, reflecting their good integration. Expansion and slight changes in peaks, especially in the ∼3400 cm−1 and ∼ 1600–1700 cm−1 regions, indicate the formation of hydrogen bonds and interactions between Ni-MOF and S-CQDs. The presence of sulfur-functional groups is reflected in the peak at about 1100 cm−1, while changed metal-ligand vibrations below 1000 cm−1 indicate the change in coordination due to the interaction between the two components. These changes, both in vibrational frequencies and the nature of the integrated spectrum, prove the successful synthesis of the Ni-MOF@S-CQDs composite material. Strong chemical interactions involving hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and even covalent bonding further enhance the functional synergy in this composite, probably resulting in an improvement of catalytic, optical, or adsorption performances.

In energy-dispersive X-ray diffraction (Fig. 2B), the well-defined peaks that are assigned to graphitic carbon (or sp2 hybridized forms) are shown in the range of 25–30°. A representative XRD pattern of Ni-MOF with major peaks of Ni coordination with organic ligands is typically found at 10–20° for primary reflections of MOFs. Ni-MOF/S-CQDs: The coexistence of both surface peaks could also occur in the compound, so it is shown in the figure that this compound has the characteristics of both parental materials. The XRD patterns of S-CQDs, Ni-MOF, and the combination of the two, Ni-MOF/S-CQDs, demonstrate the crystalline structures of the so-called “dot” materials. The peak at around 20°, which is S-CQDs' characteristic peak (John, Abraham, & Mathew, 2022), is a sign of the presence of graphitic carbon. The regular lining up of metal-organic frameworks is the reason why the sharp diffraction peaks that are located at lower angles (10–20°) are seen in Ni-MOF (Rasal, Dhavan, Jadhav, & Shimpi, 2020). However, the fact that the peaks characteristic of Ni-MOF@S-CQDs are still visible, as shown in Fig. 2B, means that the creation was indeed successful.

To analyze the particle distribution, structure integrity, and surface texture of an adsorbent, SEM images were taken at various magnifications. Fig. 3A(a-c) represents the SEM images of S-CQDs at 2 μm,1 μm, and 200 nm, respectively, while (d-f) represent Ni-MOF and (g-i) represent the composite having both Ni-MOF@S-CQDs. Images prominently represent that the composite holds both Ni-MOF and S-CQDs in its structure. To confirm the presence of key elements in the Ni-MOF@S-CQDs composite, the surface elemental composition of the adsorbent was explored using the SEM-EDX technique. The results in Fig. 3B demonstrated the presence of key elements within the synthesized adsorbent. The significant peak of S and Ni in the composite demonstrated that the composite has a homogenous distribution of elements within its porous structure, which enhances its activity more than a single material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of Pb (II) interaction with Ni-MOF@S-CQDs@Foam
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between Pb (II) ions and the Ni-MOF/S-CQDs-based polystyrene foam adsorbent is enhanced by various physicochemical mechanisms. The Ni-MOF framework provides several coordinative binding sites, mainly via NiO and NiN bonds derived from the carboxylate and amine functional groups of the organic linkers. The process of chelating Pb (II) is enhanced through coordination bonding at these specific sites. Furthermore, the incorporation of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) results in the introduction of additional –OH, –COOH, and –SO3H functional groups, thereby enhancing electrostatic attraction and complexation at near-neutral pH conditions. The electron-donating ability of the material is markedly enhanced by S-doping, facilitating partial redox-assisted adsorption. The protonation of surface groups in acidic matrices, such as fruit juice or iced tea, may decrease electrostatic interactions. Nonetheless, the ligand-assisted complexation with PADAP mitigates this impact by generating stable Pb–PADAP complexes that are firmly integrated within the adsorbent matrix. The stability of the interaction may be enhanced by π–π stacking and hydrogen bonding between the aromatic domains of PADAP and S-CQDs, while the porous structure of the polystyrene foam facilitates the efficient transport of Pb (II) ions into the active regions. Even in complex sample matrices, the integrated synergistic binding mechanisms enhance selectivity and efficiency.

The pH of the sample solution significantly affects the adsorption of Pb (II) by modifying the surface charge of the Ni-MOF@S-CQDs@foam adsorbent and the speciation of Pb (II) ions in the solution. In this section, “recovery (%)” refers to extraction recovery, defined as the percentage of Pb (II) that is retained and eluted from spiked solutions under specified extraction conditions. Fig. 4a illustrates that recovery escalated with increasing pH, peaking at pH 7. This tendency corresponds with the interaction processes outlined in Section 3.1.1, wherein deprotonated surface functional groups (–COOH, –OH, –SO3H) augment electrostatic attraction and complexation with Pb2+ ions. At reduced pH levels (e.g., pH 3–5), the surface undergoes heightened protonation, diminishing the availability of negative sites and facilitating competitive interaction with H+ ions. Simultaneously, Pb (II) primarily exists in its unbound ionic state (Pb2+) until pH 7, reducing hydrolysis and enhancing complexation capacity. Above pH 7, the slight decrease in extraction may be attributed to the initial formation of Pb (OH)+ and Pb (OH)2, which show reduced interaction with the adsorbent surface. As a result, pH 7 represents an optimal point where surface chemistry and Pb (II) speciation favor selective adsorption.

The quantity of adsorbent dictates the number of accessible active sites for Pb (II) retention. Augmenting the dosage from 5 to 20 mg improved recovery owing to an expanded surface area and greater binding capacity. Nevertheless, additional increments above 20 mg resulted in a minor decrease, potentially attributable to aggregation or diminished flow-through efficiency within the pipette tip, causing a reduction in analyte diffusion rate. Consequently, 20 mg was selected as ideal, guaranteeing elevated extraction efficiency while maintaining system dynamics (Fig. 4b).

A ligand is a molecule or ion that binds to a central metal to form a coordination complex. In this study, we used a ligand named PADAP, which formed the complex with the analyte Pb. This complex can be efficiently separated or detected via UV-VIS spectrometry. An ideal amount of ligand is also very important for extraction. To find the best concentration of PADAP, we used different amounts ranging from 0 to 30 μg mL−1. The maximum complex formed via the use of 10 μg mL−1 (Fig. 4c).

The number of adsorption cycles was chosen to maximize analyte retention while avoiding unnecessary prolongation of the procedure. Fig. 4d illustrates that recovery (%) progressively increased from 1 to 3 cycles, attaining around 95 %. A minor increase was noted after conducting a fourth cycle, indicating that some residual Pb (II) remained available for extraction. The selection of the fourth cycle, despite a minor increase from the third, was made to ensure quantitative extraction and optimal reproducibility, especially in trace-level applications. A decrease was observed after four cycles, likely due to over-processing and analyte desorption. Thus, four adsorption cycles were determined to be the optimal condition for subsequent studies, balancing extraction efficiency with methodological robustness.

To determine the optimal number of desorption cycles for the quantitative recovery of Pb (II), experiments were performed with 1 to 10 repetitions using the selected eluent (3.0 M HNO3). Fig. 4e illustrates that recovery values stabilized between 4 and 8 cycles, signifying that the majority of Pb (II) was effectively eluted during the initial stages. Following ten cycles, a marginal enhancement in recovery was noted, maybe attributable to increased disintegration of the residual Pb–ligand or Pb–sorbent interactions. Desorption cycles beyond 10 were not assessed due to the perceived little benefits and potential extension of processing durations. Ten cycles were determined to be the optimal condition for further research to enhance efficiency and achieve complete desorption.

Because it affects the interaction between the analyte and the adsorbent as well as the achievable preconcentration factor, the volume of the sample solution plays a critical role in determining the effectiveness of the extraction process. Under ideal circumstances, quantities ranging from 5 to 30 mL were used to investigate the impact of sample volume on Pb (II) recovery. As shown in Fig. 4f, the recovery percentage increased as the sample volume increased from 5 to 15 mL, likely due to enhanced interaction between the analyte and the adsorbent surface. Beyond 15 mL, the recovery started to decline, probably due to dilution effects and limited availability of active sites for binding the dispersed analyte. The recovery at 10 mL was slightly higher, and quantitative recovery was achieved at 15 mL. Thus, 15 mL was identified as the ideal sample volume for ensuring full extraction, while improving sample throughput and total preconcentration efficiency. This volume was employed for all the following studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Type, concentration, and volume of the eluent
ce:section.ce:para: The desorption of Pb (II) is influenced by the eluent's capacity to interfere with the interactions between the analyte, ligand, and sorbent. Of the eluents tested (HCl, HNO3, and acetone), HNO3 showed the greatest effectiveness, probably because of its strong protonation capability and its appropriateness for PADAP-metal complexes. Elevating HNO3 concentration enhanced desorption up to 3.0 M, beyond which no substantial improvement was noted. Similarly, 0.5 mL effectively quantitatively eluted Pb (II); increased quantities would not enhance recovery and would dilute the analyte. Consequently, 3.0 M HNO3 (0.5 mL) was chosen for its superior efficiency and reduced solvent usage (Fig. 4g, h, and i).

The ability to reuse resources is essential for economic and sustainable analysis. 3.0 M HNO3 was identified as the most effective eluent for the quantitative desorption of Pb (II); however, its high acidity likely limited the reusability of the Ni-MOF@S-CQDs@foam composite. The acidic environment has the potential to cause partial disintegration of the MOF structure, disrupt metal-ligand interactions, or result in minor deterioration of the polystyrene foam substrate. The reduction in extraction efficiency observed after one regeneration cycle is due to structural instability resulting from repeated exposure to acid. Under repeated elution with 3.0 M HNO3, the performance of the composite sorbent significantly declined after one use. The structural degradation of the Ni-MOF@S-CQDs on the foam substrate or partial leaching of the active components can be identified as factors that limit the reusability of the adsorbent. Future research may investigate chemical stabilization methods or surface cross-linking to improve recyclability (Fig. 4k).

Matrix ions affect the extraction efficiency of target analytes. The objective of the above study is that the presence of cationic and anionic substances affects the efficiency of target analytes. Foreign ions or molecules that oscillate more than 5 % in analytical signals are termed interfering agents. Supporting Data Table S1 demonstrates that a precise percentage of ions or molecules tested did not affect the analyte.

Although key interfering ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Al3+ were evaluated in this study, the potential effect of other soft or borderline heavy metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Sn2+, and Au3+) should be investigated in future studies to further assess the selectivity of the proposed method in more complex matrices.

Under ideal conditions, the proposed method demonstrated excellent analytical performance, as shown by the figures of merit listed in Supporting Data Table S2. In subsequent sections, recovery studies in spiked water, iced tea, and juice samples further support the method's effectiveness for Pb (II) extraction in real matrices. The calibration curves were constructed using seven standard solutions containing various concentrations of Pb (II) in the range of 50–5000 μg L−1. The limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) were obtained at 15 μg L−1 and 49.5 μg L−1 in the analyte, respectively. The LOD and LOQ ranges validate the proposed method's strong detection capabilities, enabling the identification of trace levels of Pb. The Enrichment Factor for Pb was 28.5, and the Pre-concentration factor was 30. As a result, the ER% was obtained at 95. The results validated the exceptional accuracy of the proposed methodology. RSD, or relative standard deviation, can have both positive and negative impacts on the produced results, which are shown using the ± symbol.

To statistically confirm the consistency and precision of the method, a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was applied to replicate recovery results at three spiking levels (0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 mg L−1) in two types of water and two types of juice samples. The ANOVA indicated no significant differences among the groups (p > 0.05), suggesting that the method consistently and reliably recovers varying concentrations. Additionally, 95 % confidence intervals were calculated for each recovery percentage (see Table S3 in the Supporting Information). The method's robustness was demonstrated by the minimal intervals that remained within acceptable analytical variation.

In 2022, the AGREE prep tool was initially used as a practical software tool for measuring the greenness of a methodology. This tool emphasized sample preparation in the investigation. The tools are based on ten distinct effect categories with sub-scores of 0–1 and elaborate the results with a distinct hue of spanning spectrum, including green, yellow, and red. The sorts of data include energy consumption, sample volume, eluent volume, trash generation, trash recycling, and usage of hazardous reagents. A pictogram that captures the strategy's overall environmental friendliness is produced by the evaluation process. The pictogram's numerous parts make it possible to compare methods and highlight the advantages and disadvantages of various strategies (Wojnowski, Tobiszewski, Pena-Pereira, & Psillakis, 2022). The AGREE prep program assigned a greenness grade of 0.63 to the recommended procedure, as shown in Fig. 5a.

For further sustainability analysis, a modified tool named Complex MoGAPI (Fig. 5b) has been utilized. With the help of this upgraded software, a comprehensive visual assessment of methodology, including greenness, safety, hazards, waste, waste management, and reagent nature. Assigning a score to each step according to the greenness of methods was illustrated using a pictogram. Moreover, when steps differ greatly, it enhances the ability to compare approaches based on their sum scores. Furthermore, the program simplifies the Complex MoGAPI application, increasing its speed and user-friendliness (Mansour, Omer, & Płotka-Wasylka, 2024).

Meanwhile, the BAGI framework (Fig. 5c) represents a new direction in the assessment of applicability and effectiveness for practical analytics methodologies. Though the GSST, SDAGI, NEMI, ComplexGAPI, and AGREE were developed to focus on the sustainability and environmental impact of the methodologies, BAGI evaluates their efficiency and adequacy for application. It evaluates practices against ten criteria as throughput, automation, and sample requirements, scored on a scale from 1.0 to 10, the geometric mean of these scores giving an overall BAGI score. This makes the review process fair because no single criterion can dominate the evaluation. The BAGI score shows the enhanced utility, efficiency, and suitability of the method for practical applications, but does not cover either sustainability or environmental issues. The BAGI score of 65 for the method underlines the remarkable application and efficiency of this method in research. This has demonstrated the benefit of the method on time and cost efficiency, reduction of risks, and effectiveness, thus being very appropriate for practical applications (Mahmoud, Abbas, & katamesh, N. S., 2024).

To validate the accuracy of the PFPT-μSPE method, the same procedure was employed on two different CRMs, including BCR 505 estuarine water and NCS-ZC73032 Cerely (Table 1 ). The certified value for Pb (II) is 2.7 μg kg−1 and 0.24 nmol kg−1, respectively. The measured values were found to be 0.23 nmol kg−1 for BCR 505 estuarine water and NCS-ZC73032 2.63 μg kg−1 with recovery values of 98 for in both CRMs. These results indicate good accuracy of the PFPT-μSPE technique.

The PFPT-μSPE method was employed on various water and food samples, including two tap water samples from the Chemistry department of Erciyes University and two battery wastewater samples from the Kayseri Industry Region, watermelon and strawberry-flavored iced tea, peach-flavored iced tea, and juices (mixed fruit and apricot juice). The samples are prepared according to the procedure in Subsection 2.5. The proposed PFPT-μSPE method was applied to samples using the standard addition method (Tables 2 and 3
). To evaluate the method's accuracy, the real samples were spiked at two levels of concentration, and the recovery values were calculated using the equation below:
(4)
RR % =
C f
−
C r
/
C added
× 100
Cf = concentration of spiked sample after extraction, Cr = concentration of analyte in the blank sample shows the actual amount of analyte present in the original real sample. Cadded = concentration of analyte added to the real sample. The results in Table 2–3 show that the proposed PFPT-μSPE technique could effectively extract Pb (II) ions from different water and food samples. The sample without standard addition has a very low concentration of Pb (II), or even in some samples, it is not determinable in some samples. Tap water, being a clean source of drinking water, is supposed to be Pb-free, but small levels of Pb (II) ranging from 0.26 to 0.30 μg mL−1 were found, with recovery values lying under the range of 86–108 %. Wastewater samples showed contamination and high levels of Pb (II). Pb (II) was not detected in an apricot juice sample, but was detected in other iced tea samples within the range of 0.03–0.04 μg mL−1, which means the amount of Pb (II) is not high in these samples. The RSD% for samples lies under 5.0 for each repetition. After applying the PFPT-μSPE method, the results confirmed the excellent accuracy for the determination of Pb (II) ions in water and food samples.
In this section, the reported recovery values correspond to relative recovery (RR%), calculated based on the difference between spiked and native concentrations to reflect accuracy in real matrices.

The novel Ni-MOF@S-CQDs strategy was examined in this work in comparison to previously documented techniques in the literature for the extraction and measurement of Pb metal ions in a range of matrices (Azam, Khan, Wabaidur, Al-Resayes, & Islam, 2022; Duman & Soylak, 2024; Gholami, Faraji, & Jafarinejad, 2023; Kori, Ahmed, & Soylak, 2024; Lorêdo de França et al., 2021). The results displayed in Table 4 indicate that, in comparison to other approaches documented in earlier publications, the suggested strategy frequently produces better LOD values. The recommended method's reproducibility is compatible with other methodologies. Additionally, compared to the suggested method, the previous methodologies are more time-consuming. The suggested method has strong analytical sensitivity and performance. Furthermore, compared to other methods, our suggested extraction adsorbent, Ni-MOF@S-CQDs, is comparatively safer. Moreover, the extraction procedure is made simpler and faster with the introduction of the Ni-MOF@S-CQDs micropipette microextraction technique, which enhances extraction recovery. For the best extraction recovery throughout the absorption and desorption stages, this strategy does not need supplementary equipment such as centrifuges and filtration devices. The suggested method has many noteworthy benefits, including its simplicity, eco-friendliness, fast examination speed, affordability, ease of use, and remarkable sensitivity.

Ni-MOF@S-CQDs foam exhibits excellent adsorption capacity, but its reusability and stability under repeated cycles need further optimization. The synthesis of material on a large scale is also challenging. The micro-pipette-based solid phase extraction method is highly effective for small-volume samples but not suitable for large sample volumes. In the future, the greener synthesis method can be explored for the synthesis of foam-based adsorbents. This type of adsorbent can be utilized for food safety purposes. Instead of Ni-MOF, different MOFs can be utilized for the synthesis of material that may enhance its efficiency.

A novel foam-based nanocomposite composed of Ni-MOF@S-CQDs was synthesized, which was applied as an effective adsorbent to strip off Pb ions from samples of iced tea, water, and other juices. For extracting Pb ions, a modified PFPT-μSPE technique was employed, followed by analysis using UV-VIS spectrometry. Firstly, Ni-MOF was synthesized using Ni (NO3)2.6H2O and PTA. To enhance the extraction ability of the synthesized material, it was treated with S-CQDs. Ni-MOF@S-CQDs have an extreme potential for the extraction of Pb metal ions. One benefit of this approach is that it's not time-consuming. Consequently, the μSPE was completed within 2 min. A technique of modifying one variable at a time was used to optimize the experimental settings. It was demonstrated that the method could be used to detect Pb metal ions in water, iced tea, and juice samples with acceptable precision and accuracy at both low-quality and high-quality control levels. In conclusion, the suggested method shows noteworthy advantages such as ease of use, simplicity, eco-friendliness, sustainability, and speedy extraction. Furthermore, the use of UV-VIS spectrometry, combined with PADAP complexation and μSPE preconcentration, enabled cost-effective and sufficiently sensitive determination of Pb (II), reinforcing the method's suitability for routine and decentralized applications. It is important to note that new adsorbents can be developed and produced to extract various emerging analytes with different properties in an efficient manner.
